blocks:
Ayahs

قَدْ سَمِعَ ٱللَّهُ قَوْلَ ٱلَّتِى تُجَـٰدِلُكَ فِى زَوْجِهَا وَتَشْتَكِىٓ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ وَٱللَّهُ يَسْمَعُ تَحَاوُرَكُمَآ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَمِيعٌۢ بَصِيرٌ (١)
Allah has certainly heard the speech of her who pleads with you about her husband and complains to Allah. Allah hears the conversation between the two of you. Indeed Allah is all-hearing, all-seeing. (1)
ٱلَّذِينَ يُظَـٰهِرُونَ مِنكُم مِّن نِّسَآئِهِم مَّا هُنَّ أُمَّهَـٰتِهِمْ ۖ إِنْ أُمَّهَـٰتُهُمْ إِلَّا ٱلَّـٰٓـِٔى وَلَدْنَهُمْ ۚ وَإِنَّهُمْ لَيَقُولُونَ مُنكَرًا مِّنَ ٱلْقَوْلِ وَزُورًا ۚ وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَعَفُوٌّ غَفُورٌ (٢)
As for those of you who repudiate their wives by zihar, they are not their mothers; their mothers are only those who bore them, and indeed they utter an outrage and a lie. Indeed Allah is all-excusing, all-forgiving. (2)
وَٱلَّذِينَ يُظَـٰهِرُونَ مِن نِّسَآئِهِمْ ثُمَّ يَعُودُونَ لِمَا قَالُوا۟ فَتَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ مِّن قَبْلِ أَن يَتَمَآسَّا ۚ ذَٰلِكُمْ تُوعَظُونَ بِهِۦ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ (٣)
Those who repudiate their wives by ³ih¡r and then retract what they have said, shall set free a slave before they may touch each other. This you are advised [to carry out], and Allah is well aware of what you do. (3)
فَمَن لَّمْ يَجِدْ فَصِيَامُ شَهْرَيْنِ مُتَتَابِعَيْنِ مِن قَبْلِ أَن يَتَمَآسَّا ۖ فَمَن لَّمْ يَسْتَطِعْ فَإِطْعَامُ سِتِّينَ مِسْكِينًا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ لِتُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ ۚ وَتِلْكَ حُدُودُ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَلِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٤)
He who cannot afford [to free a slave] shall fast for two successive months before they may touch each other. If he cannot [do so], he shall feed sixty needy persons. This, that you may have faith in Allah and His Apostle. These are Allah’s bounds, and there is a painful punishment for the faithless. (4)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يُحَآدُّونَ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ كُبِتُوا۟ كَمَا كُبِتَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ وَقَدْ أَنزَلْنَآ ءَايَـٰتٍۭ بَيِّنَـٰتٍ ۚ وَلِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَذَابٌ مُّهِينٌ (٥)
Indeed those who oppose Allah and His Apostle will be subdued just as those who passed before them were subdued. We have certainly sent down manifest signs, and there is a humiliating punishment for the faithless. (5)
يَوْمَ يَبْعَثُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ جَمِيعًا فَيُنَبِّئُهُم بِمَا عَمِلُوٓا۟ ۚ أَحْصَىٰهُ ٱللَّهُ وَنَسُوهُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ شَهِيدٌ (٦)
The day when Allah will raise them all together, He will inform them about what they have done. Allah has kept account of it, while they have forgotten, and Allah is witness to all things. (6)

(Commentary)

The surah addresses various themes including legal rulings, etiquette, and descriptions. One part deals with the rulings of zihar (الظهار) — a pre-Islamic form of divorce where a husband likens his wife to his mother's back — and secret counsel (النجوى), as well as the etiquette of sitting in assemblies. Another part describes the state of those who defy Allah and His Messenger, and those who befriend the enemies of the religion (الدين). It also describes the believers who refrain from befriending them, promising them a beautiful promise in this world and the Hereafter.

The surah is Medinan, as evidenced by the context of its verses.

His saying, the Exalted: قَدْ سَمِعَ اللهُ قَوْلَ الَّتِي تُجادِلُكَ فِي زَوْجِها وَتَشْتَكِي إِلَى اللهِ وَاللهُ يَسْمَعُ تَحاوُرَكُما "Allah has certainly heard the speech of her who pleads with you about her husband and complains to Allah, and Allah hears your conversation" (58:1) etc. It is mentioned in al-Majma‘: الاشتكاء (complaining) is to manifest the distress one feels within oneself, while الشكاية (complaint) is to manifest the distress caused by another. He said: التحاور (conversation) is the back-and-forth exchange of speech; it is said: "he conversed (حاوره) with him," meaning he exchanged words with him, and "they conversed (تحاورا)." End quote.

The first four or six verses were revealed regarding zihar (الظهار). This was a type of divorce among the Arabs of the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية). A man would say to his wife: "You are to me like the back of my mother," whereby she would be separated from him and become permanently forbidden to him. One of the Ansar (الأنصار) — the Helpers of Medina — performed zihar against his wife and then regretted it. His wife came to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to question him about it, hoping to find a way to return to him, pleading (تُجادِلُكَ) with him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله regarding that and complaining to Allah; thus, the verses were revealed.

The meaning of "hearing" (السمع) in His saying: قَدْ سَمِعَ اللهُ "Allah has certainly heard" is the answering of the prayer and the fulfillment of the need, by way of metonymy (الكناية), which is common. The evidence for this is His saying: تُجادِلُكَ فِي زَوْجِها وَتَشْتَكِي إِلَى اللهِ "who pleads with you about her husband and complains to Allah," which indicates that she was seeking a way not to be separated from her husband. As for His saying: وَاللهُ يَسْمَعُ تَحاوُرَكُما "and Allah hears your conversation," the "hearing" here is in its well-known literal sense.

The meaning is: Allah has indeed responded to the woman who pleads with you regarding her husband — who had performed zihar against her — and who complains of her grief and the ill state that has befallen her to Allah; and Allah hears your exchange of words, for Allah is All-Hearing (سميع) of sounds, All-Seeing (بصير) of all that is visible.

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ يُظاهِرُونَ مِنْكُمْ مِنْ نِسائِهِمْ ما هُنَّ أُمَّهاتِهِمْ إِنْ أُمَّهاتُهُمْ إِلَّا اللَّائِي وَلَدْنَهُمْ "As for those of you who repudiate their wives by zihar, they are not their mothers; their mothers are only those who gave birth to them" (58:2) etc. This is a negation of the ruling of zihar known among them and an annulment of its effect regarding divorce and permanent prohibition, by negating the motherhood of the wife to the husband through zihar. For the custom of the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية) would equate the wife with the mother because of zihar, making her as permanently forbidden to her husband as a mother is to her son.

Thus, His saying: ما هُنَّ أُمَّهاتِهِمْ "they are not their mothers" means according to the consideration of the Law (الشرع), such that they would be legally equated with them due to zihar and thus become permanently forbidden to them. He then emphasized this by His saying: إِنْ أُمَّهاتُهُمْ إِلَّا اللَّائِي وَلَدْنَهُمْ "their mothers are only those who gave birth to them," meaning the mothers of the husbands are none other than the women who gave birth to them.

He then emphasized this a second time by His saying: وَإِنَّهُمْ لَيَقُولُونَ مُنْكَراً مِنَ الْقَوْلِ وَزُوراً "Indeed they utter an abominable and lying word" (58:2), with a context of emphasis. That is, these husbands who perform zihar utter, through this act, an abominable (منكراً) word that the Law (الشرع) denounces, as it has neither recognized nor legislated it; and a lie (زوراً) in the sense that it does not correspond to the Law, just as it does not correspond to the external reality of existence. Thus, the verse informs us that zihar does not effect a divorce. This does not contradict the obligation of atonement (الكفارة) upon him if he intends to have intercourse after zihar; the marriage remains intact, though intercourse is forbidden before the atonement.

His saying: وَإِنَّ اللهَ لَعَفُوٌّ غَفُورٌ "Indeed Allah is All-excusing, All-forgiving" (58:2) is not devoid of an indication that it is a forgiven sin. However, the mention of atonement (الكفارة) in the following verse, concluded by His saying: وَتِلْكَ حُدُودُ اللهِ وَلِلْكافِرِينَ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ "These are the bounds of Allah, and for the faithless there is a painful punishment," perhaps indicates that forgiveness is conditional upon the atonement.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ يُظاهِرُونَ مِنْ نِسائِهِمْ ثُمَّ يَعُودُونَ لِما قالُوا فَتَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَتَمَاسَّا "Those who repudiate their wives by zihar and then retract what they have said, shall set free a slave before they touch each other" (58:3) etc. The speech is in the sense of a condition, which is why the fa (الفاء) entered the predicate (الخبر) because it carries the meaning of a recompense (الجزاء). The gist is: those who have performed zihar and then intend to return to what they said, must manumit a slave.

In His saying: مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَتَمَاسَّا "before they touch each other," there is an indication that the ruling in the verse applies to one who performed zihar and then intended to return to the state he was in before the zihar. This serves as a context (قرينة) that the meaning of يَعُودُونَ لِما قالُوا "retract what they have said" is the intention to return by undoing what they had affirmed through zihar.

The meaning is: those who perform zihar against their wives and then intend to return to the word of zihar they uttered by undoing it through intercourse, must manumit a slave before they touch each other.

It has been said that the meaning of their "returning to what they said" is their regret over the zihar. However, regret can be the resultant meaning, but not the literal meaning of the phrase يَعُودُونَ لِما قالُوا.

It has also been said that the meaning of their "returning to what they said" is their returning to the utterance of the word of zihar by pronouncing it a second time. The problem with this is that it would necessitate that the atonement (الكفارة) is always contingent upon a second zihar rather than the first, and the verse does not imply this. Furthermore, the Prophetic Tradition (السنة) only considers the occurrence of zihar itself, regardless of its repetition.

He then concluded the verse with His saying: ذلِكُمْ تُوعَظُونَ بِهِ وَاللهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ "This is what you are advised with, and Allah is well aware of what you do" (58:3), as a notification that the atonement He commanded is an exhortation from Him based on His awareness of that act of theirs. Thus, the atonement is that by which the consequence of the act is removed.

His saying, the Exalted: فَمَنْ لَمْ يَجِدْ فَصِيامُ شَهْرَيْنِ مُتَتابِعَيْنِ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَتَمَاسَّا "But he who cannot afford [that] shall fast for two successive months before they touch each other" (58:4) until the end of the verse. This is the second option of atonement (الكفارة), contingent upon the first option for one who is unable to perform it; it is the fasting of two consecutive months before they touch each other. He restricted it again with the phrase مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَتَمَاسَّا "before they touch each other" to dispel the illusion that the restriction applied only to the first option.

His saying: فَمَنْ لَمْ يَسْتَطِعْ فَإِطْعامُ سِتِّينَ مِسْكِيناً "and he who is not able [to fast] shall feed sixty needy persons" (58:4) is an explanation of the third option. Whoever cannot endure the fasting of two consecutive months must feed sixty needy persons. The detailed discussion of all this belongs to Jurisprudence (الفقه).

His saying: ذلِكَ لِتُؤْمِنُوا بِاللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ "This, so that you may have faith in Allah and His Apostle" (58:4), meaning: what We have established of the ruling and what We have obligated of the atonement — whereby We maintained the bond of marriage and imposed the atonement for whoever intends to return to intercourse as a recompense for practicing a custom from the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية) — all of this is so that you may believe in Allah and His Messenger and reject the falsehoods of those customs.

His saying: وَتِلْكَ حُدُودُ اللهِ وَلِلْكافِرِينَ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ "These are the bounds of Allah, and for the faithless there is a painful punishment" (58:4). The حد (bound/limit) of a thing is that where it ends and is not to be exceeded; its root meaning is "prevention." The meaning is that the options We have obligated or the rulings We establish are the bounds (حدود) of Allah, so do not exceed them through disobedience. For those who disbelieve in what We have judged regarding zihar or in the laws We have legislated — by way of opposition and defiance — there is a painful punishment.

It appears that "disbelief" (الكفر) here refers to the rejection of the ruling and the adherence to zihar as an effective and accepted custom. This is supported by His saying: ذلِكَ لِتُؤْمِنُوا بِاللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ "This, so that you may have faith in Allah and His Apostle," meaning that you should submit to the fact that Allah's judgment is the truth and that His Messenger is truthful and trustworthy in conveying it. He emphasized this with His saying: وَتِلْكَ حُدُودُ اللهِ "These are the bounds of Allah" etc. It is also possible that "disbelief" refers to disbelief in the realm of action, which is disobedience (العصيان).

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُحَادُّونَ اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ كُبِتُوا كَما كُبِتَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ "Indeed those who oppose Allah and His Apostle shall be confounded, just as those who were before them were confounded" (58:5) etc. المحادة (opposition) is to hinder and defy, and الكبت (confounding) is to humiliate and disgrace.

Although this verse and the one following it could be a new sentence (استئناف) explaining the matter of opposing Allah and His Messenger in terms of its consequence and effect, the apparent context suggests they are intended to provide the reason for the conclusion of the previous verse, which carries the meaning of a prohibition against opposing Allah and His Messenger. The meaning is: We have only commanded you to have faith in Allah and His Messenger and prohibited you from exceeding the bounds of Allah and disbelieving in them because those who oppose Allah and His Messenger through defiance are humiliated and disgraced just as those before them were humiliated and disgraced.

He then emphasized this by His saying: وَقَدْ أَنْزَلْنا آياتٍ بَيِّناتٍ وَلِلْكافِرِينَ عَذابٌ مُهِينٌ "We have certainly sent down manifest signs, and for the faithless there is a humiliating punishment" (58:5). That is, there is no doubt that they are from Us and that Our Messenger is truthful and trustworthy in conveying them; and for those who disbelieve in them and reject them, there is a humiliating and disgraceful punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ يَبْعَثُهُمُ اللهُ جَمِيعاً فَيُنَبِّئُهُمْ بِما عَمِلُوا "On the day when Allah will raise them all together and inform them of what they did" (58:6). This is an adverbial phrase (ظرف) for His saying: وَلِلْكافِرِينَ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ "and for the faithless there is a painful punishment." That is, for them is the painful punishment on the day Allah raises them, which is the Day of Reckoning and Recompense, and He will inform them of the reality of all they did in the world.

His saying: أَحْصاهُ اللهُ وَنَسُوهُ "Allah has kept its account, while they have forgotten it" (58:6). الإحصاء (keeping account) is to encompass the number of a thing without anything being missed. Al-Raghib said: al-ihsa’ (الإحصاء) is to obtain by number; it is said: "I counted (أحصيت) such and such," and it is derived from the word for pebbles (الحصى). Its use in this sense stems from the fact that they used to rely on pebbles for counting, just as we rely on fingers. End quote.

His saying: وَاللهُ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ شَهِيدٌ "and Allah is witness to all things" (58:6) is the reason for His saying: أَحْصاهُ اللهُ "Allah has kept its account." The explanation of Allah's being a witness (شهادة) over all things has already been mentioned at the end of Surah Ha Mim al-Sajdah.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Majah, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Hakim (who authenticated it), Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi narrated from ‘A’ishah, who said: "Blessed is He whose hearing encompasses all things. I was hearing the speech of Khawlah bint Tha‘labah, though some of it was hidden from me, while she was complaining about her husband to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, saying: 'O Messenger of Allah, he consumed my youth and I spread my womb for him — until when I became old and my childbearing ceased, he performed zihar against me. O Allah, I complain to You.' She did not leave until Gabriel descended with these verses: قَدْ سَمِعَ اللهُ قَوْلَ الَّتِي تُجادِلُكَ فِي زَوْجِها 'Allah has certainly heard the speech of her who pleads with you about her husband' — and he was Aws ibn al-Samit."

I say: The narrations from Sunni sources regarding this meaning are very numerous. They differ regarding the name of the woman, her father's name, her husband's name, and his father's name. The most well-known is that her name was Khawlah bint Tha‘labah and her husband's name was Aws ibn al-Samit al-Ansari. Al-Qummi mentioned the summary of the story in a narration, and he has another narration that will follow.

In al-Majma‘: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ يُظاهِرُونَ مِنْ نِسائِهِمْ ـ ثُمَّ يَعُودُونَ لِما قالُوا "Those who repudiate their wives by zihar and then retract what they have said." As for what the Leaders of Guidance (أئمة الهدى) — from the Progeny of Muhammad عليه‌السلام — held, it is that the meaning of "returning" (العود) is the intention for intercourse — and the undoing of the word he uttered — for intercourse is not permissible for him except after the atonement (الكفارة), and the ruling of his first word is not annulled except after the atonement.

In Tafsir al-Qummi: ‘Ali ibn al-Husayn narrated to us, saying: Muhammad ibn Abi ‘Abdillah narrated to us, from al-Hasan ibn Mahbub, from Abu Wallad, from Humran, from Abu Ja‘far عليه‌السلام, who said: "A woman from the Muslims came to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and said: 'O Messenger of Allah, so-and-so is my husband — and I have spread my womb for him and assisted him in his worldly and otherworldly affairs — he has seen nothing disliked from me, yet I complain of him to you.' He said: 'Regarding what do you complain of him?' She said: 'He said: "You are forbidden to me like the back of my mother" — and he has turned me out of my house, so look into my matter.' The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said to her: 'Allah, Blessed and Exalted, has not sent down a book in which I may judge between you and your husband — and I dislike being among those who are pretentious (المتكلفين).' So she began to weep and complain of her state to Allah عز وجل and to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and she turned to leave."

He said: "Then Allah, Blessed and Exalted, heard her pleading with the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله regarding her husband and what she complained to him, and Allah sent down Quran regarding that: بِسْمِ اللهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ ، قَدْ سَمِعَ اللهُ قَوْلَ الَّتِي تُجادِلُكَ فِي زَوْجِها — until His saying — وَإِنَّ اللهَ لَعَفُوٌّ غَفُورٌ."

He said: "So the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله sent for the woman, and she came to him. He said to her: 'Bring your husband.' She brought him, and he said to him: 'Did you say to this wife of yours: "You are forbidden to me like the back of my mother"?' He said: 'I did say that to her.' The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said to him: 'Allah, Blessed and Exalted, has sent down Quran regarding you and your wife' — and he recited: بِسْمِ اللهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ ـ قَدْ سَمِعَ اللهُ قَوْلَ الَّتِي تُجادِلُكَ — until His saying — إِنَّ اللهَ لَعَفُوٌّ غَفُورٌ. 'So take your wife back to you, for you have uttered an abominable and lying word, and Allah has excused you and forgiven you; do not repeat it.'"

He said: "The man left, regretting what he had said to his wife. Allah عز وجل disliked that for the believers thereafter and Allah sent down: الَّذِينَ يُظاهِرُونَ مِنْ نِسائِهِمْ ثُمَّ يَعُودُونَ لِما قالُوا 'Those who repudiate their wives by zihar and then retract what they have said,' meaning what the man said to his wife: 'You are to me like the back of my mother.'"

He said: "So whoever says it after Allah has excused and forgiven the first man, then upon him is فَتَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَتَمَاسَّا 'the setting free of a slave before they touch each other,' meaning intercourse. ذلِكُمْ تُوعَظُونَ بِهِ وَاللهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ ـ فَمَنْ لَمْ يَجِدْ فَصِيامُ شَهْرَيْنِ مُتَتابِعَيْنِ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يَتَمَاسَّا ـ فَمَنْ لَمْ يَسْتَطِعْ فَإِطْعامُ سِتِّينَ مِسْكِيناً 'This is what you are advised with, and Allah is well aware of what you do. But he who cannot afford [that] shall fast for two successive months before they touch each other; and he who is not able [to fast] shall feed sixty needy persons.'" He said: "So Allah made this the punishment for whoever performs zihar after the prohibition. Then He said: ذلِكَ لِتُؤْمِنُوا بِاللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ وَتِلْكَ حُدُودُ اللهِ 'This, so that you may have faith in Allah and His Apostle. These are the bounds of Allah.' He said: 'This is the bound (حد) of zihar.'" The tradition continues.

I say: The verse, given its context and especially the mention of excuse and forgiveness at its end, is more closely applicable to the story as narrated in this tradition. There is also no problem with it in terms of its chain of transmission (السند), except that it does not fully align with the literal sense of the verse's saying: الَّذِينَ يُظاهِرُونَ مِنْ نِسائِهِمْ ثُمَّ يَعُودُونَ لِما قالُوا "Those who repudiate their wives by zihar and then retract what they have said."

Ayahs

أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ مَا يَكُونُ مِن نَّجْوَىٰ ثَلَـٰثَةٍ إِلَّا هُوَ رَابِعُهُمْ وَلَا خَمْسَةٍ إِلَّا هُوَ سَادِسُهُمْ وَلَآ أَدْنَىٰ مِن ذَٰلِكَ وَلَآ أَكْثَرَ إِلَّا هُوَ مَعَهُمْ أَيْنَ مَا كَانُوا۟ ۖ ثُمَّ يُنَبِّئُهُم بِمَا عَمِلُوا۟ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ (٧)
Have you not regarded that Allah knows whatever there is in the heavens and whatever there is in the earth? There dost not takes place any secret talk among three, but He is their fourth [companion], nor among five but He is their sixth, nor when they are less than that or more but He is with them wherever they may be. Then He will inform them about what they have done on the Day of Resurrection. Indeed Allah has knowledge of all things. (7)
أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ نُهُوا۟ عَنِ ٱلنَّجْوَىٰ ثُمَّ يَعُودُونَ لِمَا نُهُوا۟ عَنْهُ وَيَتَنَـٰجَوْنَ بِٱلْإِثْمِ وَٱلْعُدْوَٰنِ وَمَعْصِيَتِ ٱلرَّسُولِ وَإِذَا جَآءُوكَ حَيَّوْكَ بِمَا لَمْ يُحَيِّكَ بِهِ ٱللَّهُ وَيَقُولُونَ فِىٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ لَوْلَا يُعَذِّبُنَا ٱللَّهُ بِمَا نَقُولُ ۚ حَسْبُهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ يَصْلَوْنَهَا ۖ فَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (٨)
Have you not regarded those who were forbidden from secret talks but again resumed what they had been forbidden from, and hold secret talks [imbued] with sin and transgression and disobedience to the Apostle? And when they come to you they greet you with words with which Allah never greeted you, and they say to themselves, ‘Why does not Allah punish us for what we say?!’ Let hell suffice them: they shall enter it, and it is an evil destination! (8)
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِذَا تَنَـٰجَيْتُمْ فَلَا تَتَنَـٰجَوْا۟ بِٱلْإِثْمِ وَٱلْعُدْوَٰنِ وَمَعْصِيَتِ ٱلرَّسُولِ وَتَنَـٰجَوْا۟ بِٱلْبِرِّ وَٱلتَّقْوَىٰ ۖ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ٱلَّذِىٓ إِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ (٩)
O you who have faith! When you converse secretly, do not hold private conversations [imbued] with sin and aggression and disobedience to the Apostle, but converse in [a spirit of] piety and Godfearing, and be wary of Allah toward whom you will be gathered. (9)
إِنَّمَا ٱلنَّجْوَىٰ مِنَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ لِيَحْزُنَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَلَيْسَ بِضَآرِّهِمْ شَيْـًٔا إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَعَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ (١٠)
Indeed [malicious] secret talks are from Satan, that he may upset the faithful, but he cannot harm them in any way except by Allah’s leave, and in Allah let all the faithful put their trust. (10)
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِذَا قِيلَ لَكُمْ تَفَسَّحُوا۟ فِى ٱلْمَجَـٰلِسِ فَٱفْسَحُوا۟ يَفْسَحِ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ ۖ وَإِذَا قِيلَ ٱنشُزُوا۟ فَٱنشُزُوا۟ يَرْفَعِ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مِنكُمْ وَٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْعِلْمَ دَرَجَـٰتٍ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ (١١)
O you who have faith! When you are told, ‘Make room,’ in sittings, then do make room; Allah will make room for you. And when you are told, ‘Rise up!’ Do rise up. Allah will raise in rank those of you who have faith and those who have been given knowledge, and Allah is well aware of what you do. (11)
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِذَا نَـٰجَيْتُمُ ٱلرَّسُولَ فَقَدِّمُوا۟ بَيْنَ يَدَىْ نَجْوَىٰكُمْ صَدَقَةً ۚ ذَٰلِكَ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ وَأَطْهَرُ ۚ فَإِن لَّمْ تَجِدُوا۟ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١٢)
O you who have faith! When you converse privately with the Apostle, offer a charity before your private talk. That is better for you and purer. But if you cannot afford [to make the offering], then Allah is indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful. (12)
ءَأَشْفَقْتُمْ أَن تُقَدِّمُوا۟ بَيْنَ يَدَىْ نَجْوَىٰكُمْ صَدَقَـٰتٍ ۚ فَإِذْ لَمْ تَفْعَلُوا۟ وَتَابَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْكُمْ فَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ (١٣)
Were you dismayed by having to offer charities before your private talks? Since you did not do it, and Allah has excused you [for not be able to comply], now maintain the prayer and pay the zakat, and obey Allah and His Apostle. Allah is well aware of what you do. (13)

(Commentary)

These verses concern private conversation (النجوى) and certain etiquettes of gathering.

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ اللهَ يَعْلَمُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ "Have you not regarded that Allah knows whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth?" (58:7). The interrogation is disavowing (إنكاري), and "regarding" (الرؤية) here refers to certain knowledge (العلم اليقيني) by way of metaphor. This sentence serves as a preamble and a justification for what follows—namely, that the Exalted is present with those engaged in private conversation, sharing in their discourse.

His saying, the Exalted: ما يَكُونُ مِنْ نَجْوى ثَلاثَةٍ إِلَّا هُوَ رابِعُهُمْ وَلا خَمْسَةٍ إِلَّا هُوَ سادِسُهُمْ "There is no private conversation between three but He is their fourth, nor between five but He is their sixth" (58:7) until the end of the verse. Private conversation (النجوى) is a verbal noun meaning to confer secretly (التناجي), which is whispering (المسارة). The singular pronouns refer to Allah, the Glorified. The meaning of His saying "their fourth" (رابِعُهُمْ) and "their sixth" (سادِسُهُمْ) is that He makes the three into four and the five into six by His participation with them in the knowledge of what they are secretly discussing, and His presence with them in observing what they whisper. This is evidenced by the context surrounding the speech: His saying at the beginning of the verse, "Have you not regarded that Allah knows...", and at its end, "Indeed Allah has knowledge of all things."

His saying: وَلا أَدْنى مِنْ ذلِكَ وَلا أَكْثَرَ "nor less than that, nor more" (58:7) means neither fewer than the mentioned numbers nor more. With these two phrases, the speech encompasses the number of those in private conversation, whatever it may be. As for "less than that," the less of three is two, and the less of five is four. As for "more," the more of five is six and above.

Among the subtleties of the verse's composition is the sequential arrangement of the numerical ranks mentioned: three, four, five, and six, without repetition. He did not say: "There is no private conversation of three but He is their fourth, nor of four but He is their fifth," and so on.

His saying: إِلَّا هُوَ مَعَهُمْ أَيْنَ ما كانُوا "but He is with them wherever they may be" (58:7). What is meant here is "withness" (المعية) in terms of knowledge of what they are secretly discussing and His participation with them in it.

From this, it becomes clear that the meaning of the Exalted being the fourth of three and the sixth of five is His withness with them in knowledge and His participation in observing what they whisper, not His being like them in completing a count. For each of them is a single corporeal person (شخص جسماني) who, by joining another like him, becomes the number two, and with two others, three. Allah, the Glorified, is transcendent above corporeality (الجسمية) and free from materiality (المادية).

This is because the context requires that the exception from His saying, "There is no private conversation...", etc., carries a single meaning: that no private conversation is hidden from Allah. Thus, His saying, "but He is their fourth" and "but He is their sixth" carries the same meaning as His saying, "but He is with them." This is the withness of knowledge (المعية العلمية)—meaning He shares in the knowledge and is concurrent with them in it—or the withness of existence (المعية الوجودية), in the sense that whenever a group is assumed to be conferring secretly, Allah, the Glorified, is there, All-Hearing, All-Knowing.

In His saying, أَيْنَ ما كانُوا "wherever they may be" (58:7), there is a generalization regarding place. Since His withness with them is in terms of knowledge and not through corporeal proximity, the situation does not vary or differ with the change of locations, whether near or far. Allah, the Glorified, is not absent from any place, yet He is not contained in any place.

From what has preceded, it also becomes clear that what the verse conveys regarding His withness with the people of private conversation—being the fourth of three and the sixth of five—does not contradict what was previously detailed in the commentary on His saying: لَقَدْ كَفَرَ الَّذِينَ قالُوا إِنَّ اللهَ ثالِثُ ثَلاثَةٍ "They are certainly faithless who say, ‘Allah is the third [person] of a trinity’" (5:73). There, it was explained that His oneness is not a numerical oneness (وحدة عددية) but a unique oneness (وحدة أحدية) with which it is impossible to assume another alongside Him as a second. Thus, the meaning of His being with them, and the fourth of three, and the sixth of five, is that He is aware of what they secretly discuss; what they hide from others is manifest and exposed to Him. It does not mean He has a limited existence (وجود محدود) that accepts counting, such that a second or third could be assumed for Him.

His saying: ثُمَّ يُنَبِّئُهُمْ بِما عَمِلُوا يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ "then He will inform them of what they did on the Day of Resurrection" (58:7) means He will inform them of the reality of the deeds they performed, including their private conversations and whisperings.

His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ عَلِيمٌ "Indeed Allah has knowledge of all things" (58:7) is a justification for His saying, "then He will inform them...", etc., and a confirmation of what preceded regarding His knowledge of what is in the heavens and the earth, and His being with the people of private conversation.

The verse serves as a preamble and preparation for the content of the subsequent verses, and its conclusion carries a severe tone related to the blame and threat found in the following verses.

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ نُهُوا عَنِ النَّجْوى ثُمَّ يَعُودُونَ لِما نُهُوا عَنْهُ "Have you not regarded those who were forbidden from secret talk, but again they return to what they were forbidden?" (58:8) until the end of the verse. The context of the verses indicates that a group of hypocrites (المنافقون) and those in whose hearts is a sickness (الذين في قلوبهم مرض) among the believers had spread private conversation among themselves in opposition to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and the believers. They would confer secretly regarding sin, aggression, and disobedience to the Messenger to hurt the believers and cause them grief. They persisted in this without ceasing despite being forbidden, so these verses were revealed.

Thus, His saying, "Have you not regarded those who were forbidden from secret talk, but again they return to what they were forbidden?" is a censure and rebuke of them in their absence. He addressed the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and did not address them directly to emphasize the contemptibility of their affair and to distance them from the honor of being addressed.

The meaning is: Have you not looked at those who were forbidden from conferring secretly among themselves about things that distress and grieve the believers, yet they return to the secret talk they were forbidden from, time and again? The use of the expression "they return" (يَعُودُونَ) indicates persistence. The shift from the pronoun of "private conversation" to the relative clause "to what they were forbidden" (لِما نُهُوا عَنْهُ)—rather than saying "they return to it"—indicates the reason for the censure and the evil of the return, as it is something prohibited.

His saying: يَتَناجَوْنَ بِالْإِثْمِ وَالْعُدْوانِ وَمَعْصِيَةِ الرَّسُولِ "conferring secretly for sin and aggression and disobedience to the Apostle" (58:8). The contrast between these three things—sin, aggression, and disobedience to the Messenger—suggests that sin (الإثم) refers to an act whose evil effect does not transcend the doer himself, such as drinking wine, gambling, or neglecting prayer, among the sins related to the rights of Allah. Aggression (العدوان) is an act involving transgression against others, which harms and annoys people, among the sins related to the rights of people. Both categories—sin and aggression—are acts of disobedience to Allah. Disobedience (معصية) to the Messenger is to oppose him in matters that are permissible in themselves, where there is no command or prohibition from Allah, but the Messenger commanded or forbade them for the welfare of the community by virtue of his authority over their affairs; for the Prophet has a greater claim over the believers than they have over themselves. An example is his forbidding them from private conversation even if it did not involve a sin.

The preceding part, "those who were forbidden from secret talk, but again they return to what they were forbidden," was a censure of them for the act of private conversation itself, insofar as it was forbidden, regardless of whether it involved a sin or not. This section, "conferring secretly for sin and aggression and disobedience to the Apostle," is a censure of them for the types of disobedience their secret talk contained. These people were the hypocrites and those with diseased hearts; they frequently engaged in private conversation among themselves to cause the believers grief, sorrow, and distress.

It is also said that the hypocrites and the Jews used to confer secretly with one another to grieve the believers, cast fear and panic among them, and weaken their resolve. However, there is some obscurity in whether the phrase "those who were forbidden from secret talk..." includes the Jews.

His saying: وَإِذا جاؤُكَ حَيَّوْكَ بِما لَمْ يُحَيِّكَ بِهِ اللهُ "And when they come to you, they greet you with that with which Allah never greeted you" (58:8). For Allah greeted him with peace (التسليم) and legislated that for him as a blessed and good greeting from Allah, yet they greeted him with something else. It is said these were the Jews; when they came to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, they would say, "Death be upon you" (السام عليك)—and al-sam (السام) means death—making it sound as if they were saying, "Peace be upon you" (السلام عليك). However, this interpretation is not without issue, as the pronouns in "they come to you" and "they greet you" refer to the relative clause "those who were forbidden from secret talk," and as mentioned, it is unclear if this includes the Jews.

His saying: وَيَقُولُونَ فِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ لَوْ لا يُعَذِّبُنَا اللهُ بِما نَقُولُ "and say to themselves, ‘Why does not Allah punish us for what we say?’" (58:8) is coordinated with "they greet you" or is a circumstantial clause (حال). Its apparent meaning is that this was internal monologue, something they harbored in their hearts. It is an incitement (تحضيض) intended for slander and mockery. Thus, it would be from the hypocrites, denying the messengership of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله by way of allusion. The meaning is: they greet you with what Allah did not greet you with, while they say to themselves—as evidenced by their statement "Why does not Allah punish us"—that you are not a messenger from Allah; for if you were His messenger, He would have punished them for what they said.

It is also said that "they say to themselves" means they say it among themselves, one speaking to another, but this is somewhat far-fetched.

Allah refuted their argument in saying "Why does not Allah punish us for what we say?" with His saying: حَسْبُهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ يَصْلَوْنَها فَبِئْسَ الْمَصِيرُ "Hell shall suffice them: they shall enter it, and it is an evil destination" (58:8). That is, they are mistaken in their denial of punishment; they shall be punished with what has been prepared for them, which is Hell, which they shall enter and whose heat they shall suffer. It is sufficient as a punishment for them.

It seems that when the hypocrites and those associated with them did not cease despite these prohibitions and stern warnings, the following was revealed: لَئِنْ لَمْ يَنْتَهِ الْمُنافِقُونَ وَالَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ وَالْمُرْجِفُونَ فِي الْمَدِينَةِ لَنُغْرِيَنَّكَ بِهِمْ ثُمَّ لا يُجاوِرُونَكَ فِيها إِلَّا قَلِيلاً ، مَلْعُونِينَ أَيْنَما ثُقِفُوا أُخِذُوا وَقُتِّلُوا تَقْتِيلاً "If the hypocrites and those in whose hearts is a sickness and the rumor-mongers in Madinah do not desist, We will surely rouse you against them, then they will not be your neighbors in it except for a little [while]. Accursed, wherever they are faced, they shall be seized and put to death." (33:60-61).

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذا تَناجَيْتُمْ فَلا تَتَناجَوْا بِالْإِثْمِ وَالْعُدْوانِ وَمَعْصِيَةِ الرَّسُولِ "O you who have faith! When you converse privately, do not converse for sin and aggression and disobedience to the Apostle" (58:9), etc. The context of the verses indicates that this verse was revealed to remove the danger. The believers were addressed and permitted to have private conversations, provided they were not for sin, aggression, or disobedience to the Messenger, but rather for righteousness (البر) and godwariness (التقوى). Righteousness (البر)—which is expansiveness in doing good—stands in contrast to aggression, and godwariness (التقوى) stands in contrast to sin. Then the speech was emphasized by a command for absolute godwariness, warning them of the Gathering (الحشر) with His saying: وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ الَّذِي إِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ "and be wary of Allah, toward whom you will be gathered" (58:9).

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّمَا النَّجْوى مِنَ الشَّيْطانِ لِيَحْزُنَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَلَيْسَ بِضارِّهِمْ شَيْئاً إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللهِ "Secret talk is only from Satan, so that he may grieve the faithful, but he cannot harm them in any way except by Allah’s leave" (58:10), etc. What is meant by "secret talk" (النجوى)—as indicated by the context—is the secret talk prevalent in those days among the hypocrites and those with diseased hearts. It is from Satan, for he is the one who beautifies it in their hearts to use it as a means to grieve the believers and disturb their hearts, making them imagine that it concerns some calamity that has befallen them or a trial that has struck them.

Then Allah, the Glorified, comforted the hearts of the believers by reminding them that the matter belongs to Allah, and that Satan or the secret talk cannot harm them at all except by Allah's leave. So let them rely on Him and not fear its harm. He, the Glorified, has explicitly stated in His saying: وَمَنْ يَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللهِ فَهو حَسْبُهُ "And whoever puts his trust in Allah, He will suffice him" (65:3) that He suffices whoever relies on Him. He urged them toward reliance (التوكل) as it is a requisite of a believer's faith; if they are believers, let them rely on Him, and He will suffice them. This is the meaning of His saying: وَلَيْسَ بِضارِّهِمْ شَيْئاً إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ اللهِ وَعَلَى اللهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ الْمُؤْمِنُونَ "but he cannot harm them in any way except by Allah’s leave, and in Allah let the faithful put their trust" (58:10).

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذا قِيلَ لَكُمْ تَفَسَّحُوا فِي الْمَجالِسِ فَافْسَحُوا يَفْسَحِ اللهُ لَكُمْ "O you who have faith! When you are told, ‘Make room,’ in assemblies, then make room; Allah will make room for you" (58:11), etc. Al-tafassuh (التفسح) means to make space, as does al-fash (الفسح). Al-majalis (المجالس) is the plural of majlis, a noun of place. Making space in a gathering means the seated person moves to accommodate another. Allah making room for him means He will expand his place in Paradise.

The verse contains an etiquette of social interaction. It is inferred from the context that they used to attend the Prophet's صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله assembly and sit in a crowded manner, leaving no room for others who arrived to sit. Thus, they were disciplined by His saying: "When you are told, ‘Make room...’", etc. The ruling is general, even if the occasion of revelation was the Prophet's صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله assembly.

The meaning is: O you who have faith, when you are told to expand in the gatherings so that the place may accommodate others with you, then expand; Allah will expand for you in Paradise.

His saying: وَإِذا قِيلَ انْشُزُوا فَانْشُزُوا "and when you are told, ‘Rise up,’ then rise up" (58:11) contains another etiquette. Al-nushuz (النشوز)—as has been said—is to rise from something by leaving it. Rising from a gathering means a person stands up from his seat so that someone else may sit there, out of respect for him and humility toward his merit.

The meaning is: And when you are told to stand so that someone more virtuous than you in knowledge or godwariness may sit in your place, then stand.

His saying: يَرْفَعِ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْكُمْ وَالَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ دَرَجاتٍ "Allah will raise those of you who have faith and those who have been given knowledge in rank" (58:11). There is no doubt that the necessary consequence of the Exalted raising the rank of one of His servants is an increase in proximity to Him. This is a rational indicator that what is meant by "those who have been given knowledge" are the scholars among the believers. Thus, the verse indicates the division of believers into two groups: the believer, and the learned believer. The learned believer is superior, as the Exalted said: هَلْ يَسْتَوِي الَّذِينَ يَعْلَمُونَ وَالَّذِينَ لا يَعْلَمُونَ "Are those who know equal to those who do not know?" (39:9).

It becomes clear from this that the raising of ranks mentioned in the verse is specific to those who were given knowledge. For the rest of the believers, there remains a raising of one rank. The implied meaning would be: Allah raises those of you who have faith by a rank, and He raises those of you who have been given knowledge by ranks.

In the verse, there is an obvious glorification of the scholars' affair and an elevation of their status. The ruling was emphasized by the conclusion of the verse: وَاللهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ "and Allah is well aware of what you do" (58:11).

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا إِذا ناجَيْتُمُ الرَّسُولَ فَقَدِّمُوا بَيْنَ يَدَيْ نَجْواكُمْ صَدَقَةً "O you who have faith! When you consult the Apostle in private, offer a charity before your consultation" (58:12), etc. That is, if you intend to confer privately with the Messenger, give charity before it.

His saying: ذلِكَ خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ وَأَطْهَرُ "That is better for you and purer" (58:12) is a justification for the legislation, similar to His saying: وَأَنْ تَصُومُوا خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ "and that you fast is better for you" (2:184). There is no doubt that its being "better for them and purer" means it is better for their souls and purer for their hearts. Perhaps the reason for this is that the wealthy among them used to frequently confer privately with the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, displaying a kind of proximity to him and exclusivity with him. This would grieve the poor among them and break their hearts. Thus, they were commanded to give charity before their private consultation to their poor, which involves the bonding of souls, the stirring of mercy, compassion, and affection, and the mending of hearts by the removal of resentment and anger.

In His saying "That" (ذلِكَ), there is a shift (التفات) to addressing the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله between two addresses to the believers. In this is a subtle honoring of him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, as the ruling of charity is linked to his صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله private consultation, and the shift to him in what pertains to him in the speech shows extra care for him.

His saying: فَإِنْ لَمْ تَجِدُوا فَإِنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ "but if you do not find [the means], then Allah is indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful" (58:12). That is, if you do not find anything to give in charity, then it is not obligatory for you to offer it. Allah has permitted you to confer with him and has pardoned you; indeed, He is All-Forgiving, All-Merciful. Thus, His saying "then Allah is indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful" places the cause in the position of the effect.

In this is an indication of the removal of the obligation from those who lack means, just as it is an indicator that the command "offer..." was intended as an obligation (الوجوب) for those with means.

His saying, the Exalted: أَأَشْفَقْتُمْ أَنْ تُقَدِّمُوا بَيْنَ يَدَيْ نَجْواكُمْ صَدَقاتٍ "Are you afraid of offering charities before your consultation?" (58:13), etc. This verse abrogates (ناسخة) the ruling of charity mentioned in the previous verse. It contains a severe reproach for the companions of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and the believers, as they ceased conferring with him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله out of fear of spending wealth in charity. None of them conferred with him except Ali (peace be upon him); he conferred with him ten times, and every time he did so, he offered a charity before his consultation. Then the verse was revealed and abrogated the ruling.

Al-ishfaq (الإشفاق) is fear. His saying "of offering..." is its object. The meaning is: Did you fear giving charity and spending wealth for the private consultation? It is also possible that the object is omitted, the meaning being: Did you fear poverty because of spending wealth?

Some have said: "Charities" (صدقات) was used in the plural because the fear was not actually from offering a single charity—as that is not a likely cause of poverty—but from the continuation of the matter and the offering of multiple charities.

His saying: فَإِذْ لَمْ تَفْعَلُوا وَتابَ اللهُ عَلَيْكُمْ فَأَقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ وَآتُوا الزَّكاةَ "So, since you did not do it and Allah has admitted your repentance, maintain the prayer and give the zakat" (58:13), etc. That is, since you did not do what you were tasked with and Allah has returned to you with pardon and forgiveness, then remain steadfast in fulfilling the rest of the obligations, such as maintaining prayer and giving zakat.

In His saying "and Allah has admitted your repentance" (وَتابَ اللهُ عَلَيْكُمْ), there is an indication that their action was a sin and disobedience, except that the Exalted has forgiven them for it.

The fact that His saying "maintain the prayer..." is branched from His saying "So, since you did not do it..." indicates the abrogation of the ruling of charity before private consultation.

In His saying: وَأَطِيعُوا اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ "and obey Allah and His Apostle" (58:13), there is a generalization of the ruling of obedience to all other obligations by requiring absolute obedience. In His saying: وَاللهُ خَبِيرٌ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ "and Allah is well aware of what you do" (58:13), there is a kind of sternness that emphasizes the obligation of obeying Allah and His Messenger.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma': Hamzah and Ruways from Ya'qub read yantajun (ينتجون), while the rest read yatajanun (يَتَناجَوْنَ). Hamzah's reading is supported by the saying of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله regarding Ali (peace be upon him)—when some of his companions said to him: "Do you confer with him privately excluding us?"—ما أنا انتجيته بل الله انتجاه — "It was not I who conferred with him privately, but rather Allah conferred with him."

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Ahmad, Abd ibn Humayd, al-Bazzar, Ibn al-Mundhir, al-Tabarani, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in Shu'ab al-Iman narrated with a good chain from Ibn Umar: that the Jews used to say to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: "Death be upon you" (sam 'alayk), intending thereby to insult him. Then they would say to themselves: "Why does not Allah punish us for what we say?" So this verse was revealed: "And when they come to you, they greet you with that with which Allah never greeted you."

In the same source: Abd al-Razzaq, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas: regarding this verse, he said: The hypocrites used to say to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: "Death be upon you," so it was revealed.

I say: This narration is closer to being accepted than the previous one due to what was mentioned in the commentary of the verse. In a narration by al-Qummi in his Tafsir, they used to greet him by saying: "Good morning" and "Good evening," which was the greeting of the people of the Age of Ignorance (الجاهلية).

In al-Majma': regarding His saying, the Exalted: "Allah will raise those of you who have faith and those who have been given knowledge in rank." It has also been mentioned in a hadith that he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: فضل العالم على الشهيد درجة ، وفضل الشهيد على العابد درجة ، وفضل النبي على العالم درجة ، وفضل القرآن على سائر الكلام ـ كفضل الله على سائر خلقه ، وفضل العالم على سائر الناس كفضلي على أدناهم — "The merit of the scholar over the martyr is one rank, the merit of the martyr over the worshiper is one rank, the merit of the Prophet over the scholar is one rank, the merit of the Quran over all other speech is like the merit of Allah over all His creation, and the merit of the scholar over the rest of the people is like my merit over the lowest of them." Narrated by Jabir ibn Abdillah.

I say: The end of the narration is not without issue. The apparent reference of the pronoun in "the lowest of them" (أدناهم) to the people implies the consideration of ranks among people—some being higher and some intermediate. If the merit of the scholar over the rest of the people—among whom are those of high rank—is like the merit of the Prophet over the lowest of people, then the scholar would be superior to the Prophet, and this is as you see [problematic].

Unless "lowest" (أدنى) means "closest," and what is meant by "the closest of them" is the one closest to the Prophet, which is the scholar, as hinted by his saying: "and the merit of the Prophet over the scholar is one rank." Thus, the meaning would be that the merit of the scholar over the rest of the people is like my merit over the one closest to me, who is the scholar.

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Sa'id ibn Mansur, Ibn Rahawayh, Ibn Abi Shaybah, Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Hakim (who authenticated it) narrated from Ali, who said: "In the Book of Allah, there is a verse that no one acted upon before me, nor will anyone act upon it after me: the verse of private consultation: 'O you who have faith! When you consult the Apostle in private, offer a charity before your consultation.' I had a dinar, so I sold it for ten dirhams. Every time I conferred with the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, I offered a dirham before my consultation. Then it was abrogated and no one acted upon it. So there was revealed: 'Are you afraid of offering charities before your consultation?'"

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: with its chain from Abu Basir from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: I asked him about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: "When you consult the Apostle in private, offer a charity before your consultation." He said: قدم علي بن أبي طالب عليه‌السلام بين يدي نجواه صدقة ـ ثم نسخها بقوله : «أَأَشْفَقْتُمْ أَنْ تُقَدِّمُوا بَيْنَ يَدَيْ نَجْواكُمْ صَدَقاتٍ» — "Ali ibn Abi Talib (peace be upon him) offered a charity before his private consultation, then it was abrogated by His saying: 'Are you afraid of offering charities before your consultation?'"

I say: There are other narrations with this meaning from the paths of both groups [Sunni and Shi'a].

Ayahs

۞ أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ تَوَلَّوْا۟ قَوْمًا غَضِبَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِم مَّا هُم مِّنكُمْ وَلَا مِنْهُمْ وَيَحْلِفُونَ عَلَى ٱلْكَذِبِ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ (١٤)
Have you not regarded those who befriend a people at whom Allah is wrathful? They neither belong to you, nor to them, and they swear false oaths [that they are with you] and they know. (14)
أَعَدَّ ٱللَّهُ لَهُمْ عَذَابًا شَدِيدًا ۖ إِنَّهُمْ سَآءَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (١٥)
Allah has prepared a severe punishment for them. Evil indeed is what they used to do. (15)
ٱتَّخَذُوٓا۟ أَيْمَـٰنَهُمْ جُنَّةً فَصَدُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ فَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ مُّهِينٌ (١٦)
They make a shield of their oaths and bar [people] from the way of Allah; so there is a humiliating punishment for them. (16)
لَّن تُغْنِىَ عَنْهُمْ أَمْوَٰلُهُمْ وَلَآ أَوْلَـٰدُهُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ (١٧)
Their possessions and children will not avail them in any way against Allah. They shall be the inmates of the Fire and they shall remain in it [forever]. (17)
يَوْمَ يَبْعَثُهُمُ ٱللَّهُ جَمِيعًا فَيَحْلِفُونَ لَهُۥ كَمَا يَحْلِفُونَ لَكُمْ ۖ وَيَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُمْ عَلَىٰ شَىْءٍ ۚ أَلَآ إِنَّهُمْ هُمُ ٱلْكَـٰذِبُونَ (١٨)
The day when Allah will raise them all together, they will swear to Him, just like they swear to you [now], supposing that they stand on something. Look! They are indeed liars! (18)
ٱسْتَحْوَذَ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ فَأَنسَىٰهُمْ ذِكْرَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ حِزْبُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ ۚ أَلَآ إِنَّ حِزْبَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ هُمُ ٱلْخَـٰسِرُونَ (١٩)
Satan has prevailed upon them, so he has caused them to forget the remembrance of Allah. They are Satan’s confederates. Look! Indeed, it is Satan’s confederates who are the losers! (19)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يُحَآدُّونَ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥٓ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ فِى ٱلْأَذَلِّينَ (٢٠)
Indeed those who oppose Allah and His Apostle—they will be among the most abased. (20)
كَتَبَ ٱللَّهُ لَأَغْلِبَنَّ أَنَا۠ وَرُسُلِىٓ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ قَوِىٌّ عَزِيزٌ (٢١)
Allah has ordained: ‘I shall surely prevail, I and My apostles.’ Indeed Allah is all-strong, all-mighty. (21)
لَّا تَجِدُ قَوْمًا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ يُوَآدُّونَ مَنْ حَآدَّ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ وَلَوْ كَانُوٓا۟ ءَابَآءَهُمْ أَوْ أَبْنَآءَهُمْ أَوْ إِخْوَٰنَهُمْ أَوْ عَشِيرَتَهُمْ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ كَتَبَ فِى قُلُوبِهِمُ ٱلْإِيمَـٰنَ وَأَيَّدَهُم بِرُوحٍ مِّنْهُ ۖ وَيُدْخِلُهُمْ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۚ رَضِىَ ٱللَّهُ عَنْهُمْ وَرَضُوا۟ عَنْهُ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ حِزْبُ ٱللَّهِ ۚ أَلَآ إِنَّ حِزْبَ ٱللَّهِ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ (٢٢)
You will not find a people believing in Allah and the Last Day endearing those who oppose Allah and His Apostle even though they were their own parents, or children, or brothers, or kinsfolk. [For] such, He has written faith into their hearts and strengthened them with a spirit from Him. He will admit them into gardens with streams running in them, to remain in them [forever], Allah is pleased with them, and they are pleased with Him. They are Allah’s confederates. Look! The confederates of Allah are indeed felicitous! (22)

(Commentary)

The verses mention a group of hypocrites (المنافقين) who take the Jews as allies and show them affection, while they oppose Allah and His Messenger. The verses condemn them for this, threatening them with a severe punishment and wretchedness. They assert that, in the end, faith (الإيمان) in Allah and the Last Day prevents one from having affection for those who oppose Allah and His Messenger, whoever they may be. Furthermore, the verses praise the believers who disassociate themselves from the enemies of Allah, promising them a stable faith, a spirit (روح) from Allah, Paradise, and His good pleasure.

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ تَوَلَّوْا قَوْماً غَضِبَ اللهُ عَلَيْهِمْ — "Have you not regarded those who have taken for friends a people with whom Allah is at odds?" (58:14). The people with whom Allah is at odds are the Jews. The Exalted said: مَنْ لَعَنَهُ اللهُ وَغَضِبَ عَلَيْهِ وَجَعَلَ مِنْهُمُ الْقِرَدَةَ وَالْخَنازِيرَ وَعَبَدَ الطَّاغُوتَ — "those whom Allah has cursed and with whom He is at odds, and some of whom He has made into apes and swine, and worshippers of the Rebel" (5:60).

And His saying: ما هُمْ مِنْكُمْ وَلا مِنْهُمْ — "They are neither of you nor of them" (58:14). The pronoun in عَلَيْهِمْ (with whom) refers to the hypocrites, and the pronoun in مِنْهُمْ (of them) refers to the Jews. The meaning is that these hypocrites, due to their vacillation between disbelief (الكفر) and faith (الإيمان), are neither truly from among you nor from among the Jews. The Exalted said: مُذَبْذَبِينَ بَيْنَ ذلِكَ لا إِلى هؤُلاءِ وَلا إِلى هؤُلاءِ — "vacillating between this and that, neither belonging to these nor to those" (4:143).

This is their description according to their outward state. As for the reality, they are joined with those they have taken as allies. The Exalted said: وَمَنْ يَتَوَلَّهُمْ مِنْكُمْ فَإِنَّهُ مِنْهُمْ — "Any of you who takes them as friends is indeed one of them" (5:51). Thus, there is no contradiction between His saying, "They are neither of you nor of them," and His saying, "is indeed one of them."

Some have suggested that the pronoun in عَلَيْهِمْ refers to the "people" (the Jews) and the pronoun in مِنْهُمْ refers to the relative pronoun (the hypocrites). The meaning would then be: they took as allies the Jews, who are neither from among you—while you are believers—nor from among these hypocrites themselves; rather, they are outsiders, disassociated from both groups. This carries a type of condemnation, but it is a remote interpretation.

And His saying: وَيَحْلِفُونَ عَلَى الْكَذِبِ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ — "and they swear to lies while they know [it]" (58:14). That is, they swear to you upon a lie that they are believers like you, while they know they are lying in their oath.

His saying, the Exalted: أَعَدَّ اللهُ لَهُمْ عَذاباً شَدِيداً إِنَّهُمْ ساءَ ما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ — "Allah has prepared for them a severe punishment. Evil indeed is what they used to do" (58:15). Preparation (الإعداد) means to make something ready. His saying, "Evil indeed is..." is the justification for the preparation. In His saying, كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ (they used to do), there is an indication that they were persistent in their actions and constant in them.

The meaning is: Allah has prepared for them a severe punishment because of their persistence in their evil deeds.

His saying, the Exalted: اتَّخَذُوا أَيْمانَهُمْ جُنَّةً فَصَدُّوا عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ فَلَهُمْ عَذابٌ مُهِينٌ — "They make a shield of their oaths, and so they bar [people] from the way of Allah. So there is a humiliating punishment for them" (58:16). Oaths (الأيمان) is the plural of yamin (يمين), which is a sworn oath. A shield (الجنة) is a covering used to protect oneself from harm, like a buckler. Humiliating (المهين) is the active participle of ihana (إهانة), meaning to debase and disgrace.

The meaning is: they took their oaths as a cover to repel accusation and suspicion from themselves whenever something suspicious to the believers appeared from them. Thus, they turned themselves and others away from the way of Allah (سبيل الله), which is Islam. Therefore, they shall have—for that reason—a debasing and disgraceful punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: لَنْ تُغْنِيَ عَنْهُمْ أَمْوالُهُمْ وَلا أَوْلادُهُمْ مِنَ اللهِ شَيْئاً أُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ النَّارِ هُمْ فِيها خالِدُونَ — "Neither their wealth nor their children will avail them anything against Allah. They shall be the inmates of the Fire and they shall remain in it [forever]" (58:17). That is, what prompted them toward their current state was the enjoyment of the life of this world, which consists of wealth and children. However, they are in need of escaping an eternal punishment, a need that none can fulfill for them except Allah, the Glorified. They are in poverty toward Him, and neither their wealth nor their children will avail them anything against Him; so let them believe in Him and worship Him.

His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ يَبْعَثُهُمُ اللهُ جَمِيعاً فَيَحْلِفُونَ لَهُ كَما يَحْلِفُونَ لَكُمْ وَيَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُمْ عَلى شَيْءٍ — "The day when Allah will raise them all together, they will swear to Him as they swear to you, and they will suppose that they are on something" (58:18). This is an adverbial phrase for what preceded in His saying, "Allah has prepared for them a severe punishment," or for His saying, "They shall be the inmates of the Fire." His saying, "they will swear to Him as they swear to you," means they will swear to Allah on the Day of Resurrection just as they swear to you in this world.

We have previously explained in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ لَمْ تَكُنْ فِتْنَتُهُمْ إِلَّا أَنْ قالُوا وَاللهِ رَبِّنا ما كُنَّا مُشْرِكِينَ — "Then their only excuse will be to say, ‘By Allah, our Lord, we were not polytheists’" (6:23), that their swearing to a lie on the Day of Resurrection—despite the manifestation of the realities of matters then—is due to the manifestation of their inner dispositions (الملكات) there, because of their rootedness in their souls during the worldly life. They became accustomed in it to presenting falsehood over truth through false oaths. As they live, they die; and as they die, they are resurrected.

Of this nature is their request to be returned to the world on that day, their desire to exit the Fire, their quarreling in the Fire, and other such things recounted by the Noble Quran. They will witness with their own eyes that there is no way to any of that, for that day is a day of recompense, not a day of action.

As for His saying: وَيَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُمْ عَلى شَيْءٍ — "and they will suppose that they are on something," it means they think they are established upon something solid that allows them to hide the truth and prevent the manifestation of their lies through denial and false swearing.

This could be a qualification for His saying, "as they swear to you," thus referring to their description in the world—that they think their swearing to you benefits them and satisfies you. In this case, His saying: أَلا إِنَّهُمْ هُمُ الْكاذِبُونَ — "Behold! They are indeed the liars" (58:18) is a decree from Him, the Exalted, regarding them, that they are liars; thus, no attention is paid to their nonsense, nor is any weight given to their oaths.

Alternatively, it could be a qualification for His saying, "they will swear to Him," being of the nature of the manifestation of inner dispositions on that day, as mentioned regarding the meaning of their swearing just now. In this case, His saying, "Behold! They are indeed the liars," is a judgment from Him, the Exalted, of their lying on the Day of Resurrection, or absolutely.

His saying, the Exalted: اسْتَحْوَذَ عَلَيْهِمُ الشَّيْطانُ فَأَنْساهُمْ ذِكْرَ اللهِ أُولئِكَ حِزْبُ الشَّيْطانِ أَلا إِنَّ حِزْبَ الشَّيْطانِ هُمُ الْخاسِرُونَ — "Satan has prevailed over them and caused them to forget the remembrance of Allah. They are Satan’s confederates. Behold! The confederates of Satan are indeed the losers" (58:19). Prevailing (الاستحواذ) means mastery and dominance. The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُحَادُّونَ اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ أُولئِكَ فِي الْأَذَلِّينَ — "Indeed those who oppose Allah and His Apostle will be among the most abased" (58:20). This is a justification for their being the losers. That is, they were losers only because they oppose Allah and His Messenger through disobedience and stubbornness; and those who oppose Allah and His Messenger are among the most abased (الأذلين) of Allah's creation.

It is said they are among the most abased because the humiliation of one of two disputants is in proportion to the might (عزة) of the other. Since all might belongs to Allah, nothing remains for the one who opposes Him except pure humiliation.

His saying, the Exalted: كَتَبَ اللهُ لَأَغْلِبَنَّ أَنَا وَرُسُلِي إِنَّ اللهَ قَوِيٌّ عَزِيزٌ — "Allah has ordained: ‘I shall surely prevail, I and My apostles.’ Indeed Allah is all-strong, all-mighty" (58:21). Ordaining (الكتابة) is the decree (القضاء) from Him, the Exalted.

The apparent generality of "prevailing" (الغلبة) includes prevailing in terms of proof (الحجة), in terms of unseen support (التأييد الغيبي), and in terms of the nature of faith (الإيمان) in Allah and His Messenger.

As for the proof, man is created with the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — capable of perceiving the truth and submitting to it. If the truth is explained to him through a path he is familiar with, he will soon comprehend it. Once he comprehends it, his primordial nature acknowledges it and his inner self submits to it, even if he does not submit in action due to following passion or any other obstacle.

As for prevailing in terms of unseen support and the decree of truth over falsehood, it is sufficient to consider the types of punishment Allah sent down upon the deniers of past nations, such as the people of Noah, Hud, Salih, Lot, Shu'ayb, the House of Pharaoh, and others to whom He refers by saying: ثُمَّ أَرْسَلْنا رُسُلَنا تَتْرا كُلَّ ما جاءَ أُمَّةً رَسُولُها كَذَّبُوهُ فَأَتْبَعْنا بَعْضَهُمْ بَعْضاً وَجَعَلْناهُمْ أَحادِيثَ فَبُعْداً لِقَوْمٍ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ — "Then We sent Our apostles successively. Whenever there came to a nation its apostle, they would impugn him. So We made them follow one another [to destruction] and We made them [the subject of] stories. So away with a people who do not believe!" (23:44). Upon this the divine sunnah (السنة الإلهية) has proceeded, and its mention is summarized in His saying: وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَسُولٌ فَإِذا جاءَ رَسُولُهُمْ قُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ بِالْقِسْطِ وَهُمْ لا يُظْلَمُونَ — "There is an apostle for every nation. So when their apostle comes, judgement is made between them with justice, and they are not wronged" (10:47).

As for prevailing in terms of the nature of faith in Allah and His Messenger, the faith of a believer calls him to defend and protect the truth and to resist falsehood absolutely. He sees that if he kills, he is successful, and if he is killed, he is successful. Thus, his steadfastness in defense is neither conditioned by any condition nor limited by any boundary. This is unlike one who defends not the truth as truth, but rather some worldly objective; he only defends for his own sake. If he sees himself on the brink of destruction or facing great risk, he turns back in flight. He only defends on the condition of, and up to the limit of, personal safety and not being on the verge of death. It is necessary that an absolute resolve prevails over a resolve that is conditioned and limited. A witness to this is the battles of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the victory and triumph they led to, despite being alternating in fortune; they only ended in the advancement and prevalence of the Muslims.

The Islamic conquests did not cease, nor did the ranks of the Muslims scatter like the people of Saba, except due to the corruption of their intentions and the replacement of the way of godwariness (التقوى) and sincerity to Allah and the spreading of the true religion with the spreading of authority and the expansion of the kingdom. ذلِكَ بِأَنَّ اللهَ لَمْ يَكُ مُغَيِّراً نِعْمَةً أَنْعَمَها عَلى قَوْمٍ حَتَّى يُغَيِّرُوا ما بِأَنْفُسِهِمْ — "That is because Allah never changes a blessing that He has bestowed on a people unless they change what is in their own souls" (8:53). Allah had made it a condition for them, when He perfected their religion and made them secure from their enemy, that they should fear Him, as He said: الْيَوْمَ يَئِسَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ دِينِكُمْ فَلا تَخْشَوْهُمْ وَاخْشَوْنِ — "Today the faithless have despaired of your religion. So do not fear them, but fear Me" (5:3).

Sufficient to record this prevalence is His saying, the Exalted, addressing the believers: وَلا تَهِنُوا وَلا تَحْزَنُوا وَأَنْتُمُ الْأَعْلَوْنَ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ — "Do not become hollow or mourn, for you are the upper hand, if you are believers" (3:139).

His saying, the Exalted: لا تَجِدُ قَوْماً يُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ يُوادُّونَ مَنْ حَادَّ اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ وَلَوْ كانُوا آباءَهُمْ أَوْ أَبْناءَهُمْ أَوْ إِخْوانَهُمْ أَوْ عَشِيرَتَهُمْ — "You will not find a people believing in Allah and the Last Day love those who oppose Allah and His Apostle, even though they be their fathers, or their sons, or their brothers, or their kinsmen" (58:22). The negation of finding a people with this quality is an allusion to the fact that sincere faith in Allah and the Last Day does not coexist with affection for the people of opposition and stubbornness among the disbelievers, even if it is accompanied by any cause of affection such as fatherhood, sonship, brotherhood, or other types of kinship. Between faith and affection for the people of opposition, there is a contradiction (تضاد) such that they cannot coexist.

It has become clear that His saying, "even though they be their fathers..." is an indication of the causes of affection in general. Kinship affection was specifically mentioned because it is the strongest cause of affection in terms of its stability and lack of change.

And His saying: أُولئِكَ كَتَبَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمُ الْإِيمانَ — "It is they in whose hearts He has inscribed faith" (58:22). The reference is to the people with the mentioned quality. Inscription (الكتابة) is the firm establishment such that it does not change or vanish. The pronoun refers to Allah, and in this is an explicit statement that they are truly believers.

And His saying: وَأَيَّدَهُمْ بِرُوحٍ مِنْهُ — "and whom He has strengthened with a spirit from Him" (58:22). Strengthening (التأييد) is to give power. The pronoun of the subject in أَيَّدَهُمْ refers to Allah, the Exalted, as does the pronoun in مِنْهُ (from Him), where "from" denotes origin. The meaning is: Allah strengthened them with a spirit from His presence. It is also said that the pronoun refers to faith, meaning: Allah strengthened them with a spirit of the same genus as faith, by which He gives life to their hearts; there is no harm in this interpretation.

It is also said that the spirit refers to Gabriel, or the Quran, or proof and evidence. These are weak views with no evidence from the wording.

Furthermore, the spirit (الروح) — according to what is immediately understood from its meaning — is the principle of life from which power and consciousness emanate. Keeping His saying, "and whom He has strengthened with a spirit from Him," upon its literal meaning implies that the believers possess, beyond the human spirit shared by the believer and the disbeliever, another spirit that pours upon them another life, accompanied by a new power and consciousness. To this, His saying, the Exalted, refers: أَوَمَنْ كانَ مَيْتاً فَأَحْيَيْناهُ وَجَعَلْنا لَهُ نُوراً يَمْشِي بِهِ فِي النَّاسِ كَمَنْ مَثَلُهُ فِي الظُّلُماتِ لَيْسَ بِخارِجٍ مِنْها — "Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and maintained for him a light by which he walks among the people, like one whose description is that he is in a manifold darkness from which he cannot emerge?" (6:122), and His saying: مَنْ عَمِلَ صالِحاً مِنْ ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنْثى وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَلَنُحْيِيَنَّهُ حَياةً طَيِّبَةً — "Whoever acts righteously, [whether] male or female, should he be faithful, We shall surely revive him with a good life" (16:97).

The "goodness of life" mentioned in the verse necessitates the goodness of its effect, which is the power and consciousness from which righteous deeds (الأعمال الصالحة) branch out. These two are expressed in the aforementioned verse of al-An'am as "light" (النور), and similar to it is His saying: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللهَ وَآمِنُوا بِرَسُولِهِ يُؤْتِكُمْ كِفْلَيْنِ مِنْ رَحْمَتِهِ وَيَجْعَلْ لَكُمْ نُوراً تَمْشُونَ بِهِ — "O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah and believe in His Apostle. He will grant you a double share of His mercy and give you a light to walk by" (57:28).

This is a special, noble life that has specific effects inseparable from man's eternal felicity, beyond the common life shared by the believer and the disbeliever which has common effects. Thus, it has a specific principle, which is the "spirit of faith" (روح الإيمان) mentioned in the verse, beyond the spirit shared by the believer and the disbeliever.

Accordingly, there is no reason for what they mentioned—that the spirit refers to the light of the heart, which is the light of knowledge through which tranquility is attained, and that naming it "spirit" is a trope (مجاز مرسل) because it is the cause of the good eternal life, or a metaphor (استعارة) because in its accompaniment of the modes of knowledge poured upon the heart—and knowledge is the life of the heart just as ignorance is its death—it resembles the spirit that bestows life. [End of quote].

And His saying: وَيُدْخِلُهُمْ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ خالِدِينَ فِيها — "and He will admit them into gardens with conduits running amidst them, to remain in them [forever]" (58:22). This is a beautiful promise and a description of their good afterlife.

And His saying: رَضِيَ اللهُ عَنْهُمْ وَرَضُوا عَنْهُ — "Allah is well-pleased with them and they are well-pleased with Him" (58:22). This is an appositive sentence justifying His saying, "and He will admit them into gardens..." Allah's pleasure with them is His mercy toward them for their sincerity in faith to Him, and their pleasure with Him is their delight in what He has provided them of the good life and Paradise.

And His saying: أُولئِكَ حِزْبُ اللهِ أَلا إِنَّ حِزْبَ اللهِ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ — "They are Allah’s confederates. Behold! The confederates of Allah are indeed the felicitous" (58:22). This is an honoring of these sincere ones in their faith, by stating that they are His party, just as those hypocrites who ally with the enemies of Allah are the party of Satan; and these are successful, just as those are losers.

In His saying, "Behold! The confederates of Allah," the explicit noun is used in place of the pronoun so that the speech may flow like a well-known proverb.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma': Regarding His saying, the Exalted: كَتَبَ اللهُ لَأَغْلِبَنَّ أَنَا وَرُسُلِي — "Allah has ordained: ‘I shall surely prevail, I and My apostles’" (58:21). It is narrated that the Muslims said—when they saw the villages Allah was opening for them: "Allah will surely open for us Rome and Persia." The hypocrites said: "Do you think Persia and Rome are like some of the villages you have overcome?" So Allah sent down this verse.

I say: It appears that this is of the nature of applying the verse to the story, and its likes are many. For this reason, it was narrated regarding His saying, the Exalted: لا تَجِدُ قَوْماً يُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ — "You will not find a people believing in Allah and the Last Day..." (58:22) that it was revealed concerning Abu Ubaydah ibn al-Jarrah, who killed his father on the day of Badr. In some narrations: it was revealed concerning Abu Bakr when his father insulted the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—so Abu Bakr struck him a blow that he fell to the ground, and the verse was revealed. And concerning Abd al-Rahman ibn Thabit ibn Qays ibn al-Shammas—he asked the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) for permission to visit his maternal uncle from among the polytheists, and he permitted him—when he arrived, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the Muslims around him recited the verse.

These are narrations that do not harmonize with the apparent continuity within the verses.

In al-Durr al-Manthur: al-Tayalisi and Ibn Abi Shaybah narrated from al-Bara' ibn 'Azib who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: أوثق عرى الإيمان الحب في الله والبغض في الله — "The firmest handhold of faith is love for the sake of Allah and hatred for the sake of Allah."

In al-Kafi, with its chain to Aban ibn Taghlib from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) who said: ما من مؤمن إلا ولقلبه أذنان في جوفه : أذن ينفث فيها الوسواس الخناس ـ وأذن ينفث فيها الملك ـ فيؤيد الله المؤمن بالملك ـ فذلك قوله : وَأَيَّدَهُمْ بِرُوحٍ مِنْهُ — "There is no believer except that his heart has two ears within it: an ear into which the 'slinking whisperer' (الوسواس الخناس) blows, and an ear into which the angel blows. Thus Allah strengthens the believer with the angel—and that is His saying: 'and whom He has strengthened with a spirit from Him'."

I say: Its meaning is not the interpretation of the spirit as the angel; rather, the angel accompanies the spirit and acts through it. The Exalted said: يُنَزِّلُ الْمَلائِكَةَ بِالرُّوحِ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ — "He sends down the angels with the spirit by His command" (16:2).

In the same source, with its chain to Ibn Bukayr who said: I said to Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "When a man commits adultery, the spirit of faith (روح الإيمان) departs from him." He said: هو قوله : وَأَيَّدَهُمْ بِرُوحٍ مِنْهُ ذلك الذي يفارقه — "It is His saying: 'and whom He has strengthened with a spirit from Him'; that is what departs from him."

In the same source, with its chain to Muhammad ibn Sinan from Abu Khadijah who said: I entered upon Abu al-Hasan (peace be upon him) and he said to me: إن الله تبارك وتعالى أيد المؤمن بروح ـ تحضره في كل وقت يحسن فيه ويتقي ـ وتغيب عنه في كل وقت يذنب فيه ويعتدي ـ فهي معه تهتز سرورا عند إحسانه ـ وتسيخ في الثرى عند إساءته ، فتعاهدوا عباد الله نعمه بإصلاحكم أنفسكم ـ تزدادوا يقينا وتربحوا نفيسا ثمينا ، رحم الله امرءا هم بخير فعمله أو هم بشر فارتدع عنه — "Indeed Allah, Blessed and Exalted is He, has strengthened the believer with a spirit—it is present with him at every time he does good and is godwary, and it is absent from him at every time he sins and transgresses. It is with him, vibrating with joy at his good-doing, and sinking into the earth at his evil-doing. So, O servants of Allah, tend to His blessings by reforming yourselves—you will increase in certainty and gain something precious and valuable. May Allah have mercy on a person who intended good and performed it, or intended evil and refrained from it." Then he said: نحن نؤيد الروح بالطاعة لله والعمل له — "We strengthen the spirit through obedience to Allah and action for His sake."

I say: It has become clear from what preceded in the commentary on the verse that this spirit is among the levels of the human spirit attained by the believer when he perfects his faith. Thus, it is not separate from him, just as the vegetative, animal, and human spirits shared by the believer and the disbeliever are levels of his spirit and not separate from him. However, it begins as a good state (هيئة) in the soul that may vanish due to the occurrence of an evil state that opposes it, then returns when the opposing obstacles vanish, until it becomes established, rooted, and the soul is formed by it; then it remains firm and does not change.

By this, it becomes clear that the meaning of the Imam's (peace be upon him) saying, "a spirit that is present with him," and his saying, "it is with him," refers to the presence of its form as an accidental state capable of vanishing. His saying, "sinking into the earth," refers to the vanishing of the state by way of metaphor. Likewise is the saying of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in the previous narration: "the spirit of faith departs from him."
